payload.text: Let's allow the user to add a custom (non-public) API and make it smart enough to know when to fetch based on those 3 choice you have for the public ones.

**Considering Custom API Input**

I'm focusing on enabling custom API URL input within the "Via AXIOM Terminal" app. The goal is to let users specify a non-public API for market data retrieval, bypassing the default simulation or CoinGecko options. Currently the app uses a `Market` struct to manage data. I think I'll need to modify this and related methods to support dynamic API selection and configuration, and handle the potential for authentication or other custom request requirements.


**Integrating Refresh Rates**

I'm now integrating the custom API functionality with the existing refresh rate logic. It seems logical to have the "AGGRESSIVE," "STANDARD," and "SAFE" refresh rate settings apply to custom API calls as well. My current plan is to adapt the existing `SimulatorService` to handle custom API calls, ensuring the data fetching respects the selected refresh interval. I need to make sure the app is smart enough to use these refresh rates or have its own behaviors when custom API endpoint is selected. I will also make some changes on the user interface.


**Defining Custom API Input**

I'm now diving into the specifics of custom API input. I'm adding a "CUSTOM API" option in the UI, complete with a URL input field. I'm also ensuring the refresh rate settings apply to custom APIs. On the back end, the `DataSourceMode` type in the `UserPrefsService` is being updated to include 'CUSTOM'. I'll also add a signal to store the custom API URL.

\n\n
**Implementing API Fetching**

I'm updating `MarketSimulatorService`'s `performUpdate()` to handle custom API calls. When the mode is 'CUSTOM', it'll fetch data from the provided URL. I need to map the API response to the `Token` structure, assuming the API returns a JSON array of `{ symbol: string, price: number }`. I'm trying to provide a robust method and allow some flexibility in the "Technical Blueprint."


**Refining Data Mapping**

Now I'm focusing on the data mapping aspect for the custom API integration. I'll make the fetch logic in `MarketSimulatorService` more robust. I'm working with the assumption that the API responds with a format like `[{ "symbol": "SOL", "price": 145.5, "volume": 100000 }]`. I plan to fetch from the URL and iterate through the current tokens to map the data from API response.


**Establishing Data Flow**

I'm now establishing the data flow for custom API calls. I'm updating `UserPrefsService` to include a `customApiUrl` signal and a `DataSourceMode` that includes 'CUSTOM'. In `MarketSimulatorService`, I've added a new `fetchCustomData` function to handle the fetching when the mode is 'CUSTOM'. I still need to map the result to the `Token` struct and handle errors.


**Handling API Responses**

I'm now concentrating on how the application processes responses from the custom API calls. I'm focusing on ensuring robust handling of different response formats. I plan to use an array and perform case-insensitive symbol matching. In `MarketSimulatorService`'s `fetchCustomData`, I'm handling both successful and error scenarios. It also updates the existing tokens with the new prices and volumes if they exist.


**Adding Custom API Options**

I'm now incorporating a new "CUSTOM" data source option within the settings modal, using a button element. The UI updates will reflect the selected custom mode, allowing the use of the new feature. I also need to update the `configureHeartbeat` function to treat the 'CUSTOM' data source the same as the 'LIVE' one, with respect to refresh rates. I'm focusing on the implementation to make the new option fully functional.


**Implementing UI/UX Logic**

I'm now generating code based on the established workflow, focusing on user interface elements and data retrieval. I'm updating the `user-prefs.service.ts` to include 'CUSTOM' in `DataSourceMode` and adding the `customApiUrl` signal. Also, in the `market-simulator.service.ts`, I'm updating `configureHeartbeat` and `performUpdate`, and starting to implement `fetchCustomData`. The settings modal in `app.component.html` will be updated with the Custom API button and input field, with adjusted refresh rate visibility. I will handle the custom API input changes in `app.component.ts`.


**Adjusting Data Parsing**

I'm now adapting the data parsing to handle varying API response formats for custom sources. I'm focusing on the assumption of an array of objects: `{ symbol: string, price: number, volume?: number }`. I'm updating the parsing logic in `MarketSimulatorService` to accommodate this structure. Furthermore, I've generalized the refresh rate logic to include 'CUSTOM' data sources, ensuring it applies when the selected API is used, by updating `configureHeartbeat`. Now I'll prepare the XML.


**Refining Custom Preferences**

I'm now refining the `UserPrefsService`. I've added a `customApiUrl` signal to store the user's custom API endpoint and a `DataSourceMode` enum with a 'CUSTOM' option. The service also includes signals for `aiTone`, `refreshRate`, and `focusedWidget`. I'm focused on the data settings, so I can save the custom API for the next use.


**Updating Data Handling**

I'm now integrating the focus logic into my code, focusing on the click behavior to toggle widget focus. I'm importing types and making some changes in `MarketSimulatorService` to update data handling. I'm updating `configureHeartbeat` to accommodate `CUSTOM` data sources, ensuring the system treats the mode the same as `LIVE`. I also need to update my string literals. I'm checking the current `searchTokens` logic.


**Defining Search Behavior**

I'm now determining the search behavior in 'CUSTOM' mode. I've considered that arbitrary custom APIs might not have a search endpoint. I think that the search should work using CoinGecko (as in 'LIVE' mode) to discover tokens, and then rely on the custom API for the actual price data if available. Otherwise it defaults to local search to match the simulator. I will check to see how the code affects current search logic, depending on the `dataSourceMode`.


**Implementing Local Search**

I've decided to implement local search in Custom mode to avoid CORS issues and simplify the user experience. I'm focusing on ensuring the search functionality aligns with this approach and that the application retrieves and displays the correct results. My plan includes an adjustment for the `MarketSimulatorService` so it always uses the local token list in the "CUSTOM" data mode, as well as modifications to the UI to reflect this change.


**Refining Input Field**

I'm now refining the custom API input field within the settings modal to handle user input more effectively. I'm focusing on binding the input field's value to the `customApiUrl` signal using `(input)="updateCustomUrl($event)`. This setup ensures that updates to the URL field directly modify the stored custom API endpoint in the `UserPrefsService`, maintaining the live UI binding for a seamless user experience.


**Implementing Input Validation**

I'm now implementing the `updateCustomUrl` method in `AppComponent` to handle the custom API input field changes. I'm focusing on directly updating the `customApiUrl` signal within the `UserPrefsService` through a wrapper. This method ensures that updates to the URL field correctly modify the stored API endpoint and that a seamless user experience is maintained. I'm making final checks on `MarketSimulatorService.ts` search logic.


**Adjusting Search Logic**

I'm now refining the search behavior and performance, and ensuring the application retrieves and displays the correct results in the "CUSTOM" mode. I've updated the condition to include `CUSTOM`, so that local search is used when the source mode is simulation or custom. The `performUpdate` function should respect the heartbeat. I'll make `fetchCustomData` handle both array and object formats for better usability.


**Refining Data Retrieval**

I'm now refining data retrieval and error handling for custom APIs. I'm focusing on ensuring robust handling of different API response structures, including both array and object formats. I've integrated a `try...catch` block around the `fetch` call to manage network errors. I'm working to handle cases where the API returns an error status code gracefully.


**Improving Data Processing**

I'm now refining data processing within `fetchCustomData`. I've added error handling within the `try...catch` block. The parsing now handles different response formats, including single object structures and array objects. I'm focusing on parsing `price` and `volume` and updating token histories for chart visualization, ensuring graceful handling of missing or invalid data, including using a fallback value if necessary.


**Generating XML Implementation**

I'm now generating the implementation of the XML. I'll focus on consolidating updates for `src/services/user-prefs.service.ts`. I also need to update imports in `src/services/market-simulator.service.ts` if I change types, but currently I am importing values. The `DataSourceMode` type, if added, requires import in the signature. I'll need to update the imports in `market-simulator.service.ts` if types are used in method signatures. I will ensure correct importing of types and classes.

payload.text: Let's give the option for dark/light mode in settings please.

**Considering Dark/Light Toggle**

I'm currently focused on incorporating a dark/light mode toggle within the application's settings. The application is currently designed with a "Slate 950" background and dark theme colors (text-slate-200, green-500). I intend to integrate this toggle in a user-friendly manner.


**Implementing Theme Preferences**

I'm now focused on implementing theme preferences, specifically adding a theme signal ('DARK' or 'LIGHT') to `UserPrefsService`, along with a `setTheme` method. In the `AppComponent` template, I plan to bind the 'dark' class to the root container. Since I'm using the CDN version of Tailwind, modifying `tailwind.config.js` isn't feasible, so I'll utilize Tailwind's `dark:` variant strategy directly in the component's HTML. This approach ensures a direct integration of the dark/light toggle.


**Refining Dark Mode Strategy**

I'm now refining the dark mode implementation strategy. Given the CDN usage, I'll configure Tailwind directly in `index.html` to enable class-based dark mode. In `AppComponent`, I'll toggle the `dark` class on the document body. My focus has shifted to adapting existing styles; I need to update color classes (e.g., `bg-slate-950` to `dark:bg-slate-950 bg-slate-100`) to support both modes. The light mode might lean towards a "Clean Lab" aesthetic, given the original "Cyberpunk Terminal" theme.


**Implementing Class Refactor**

I'm now implementing the dark mode class refactor across the application. I'll inject `DOCUMENT` into `AppComponent` to toggle the `dark` class on the `body` within an effect. I'll also update the Settings modal in `app.component.html` to include the toggle. I'm focusing on updating the CSS classes: `bg-slate-950` is now `bg-slate-50 dark:bg-slate-950`, `bg-slate-900/50` is now `bg-white dark:bg-slate-900/50`, and `border-slate-800` is now `border-slate-200 dark:border-slate-800`. I'm updating `app.component.html` and other components.


**Simplifying Dark Mode**

I'm now simplifying the dark mode implementation to reduce changes and improve maintainability. Instead of modifying every component, I'll focus on a more streamlined approach. I'll update `index.html` to configure Tailwind for class-based dark mode, and then I'll revise `UserPrefsService` to manage a `theme` signal. I plan to modify `AppComponent` to toggle the 'dark' class on the `document.documentElement` element based on this signal, thereby controlling the dark mode globally. This should minimize the risk of errors and create a cleaner implementation.


**Adjusting Application Styles**

I'm now focusing on updating the application's styles. I will update the settings UI and add the `dark:` prefix. I'm also planning to update the following components: `intel-panel`, `market-grid`, `signal-feed`, `token-detail`, `sync-indicator`, and `logo`. Further, the `UserPrefsService` addition is straightforward. Finally, I will modify `index.html`.



**Refining Color Adjustments**

I'm now methodically updating background, text, and border classes across components. I've focused on `AppComponent`'s HTML, injecting `DOCUMENT` into `AppComponent` to toggle the dark class on `document.documentElement` within an effect. I'm transforming classes like `bg-slate-950` to `bg-slate-50 dark:bg-slate-950` and applying appropriate light-mode values. I'm focusing on consistency in `bg-`, `text-`, and `border-` mappings, refining the color transitions. The Settings modal UI has been updated to include the theme switch. I'm moving sequentially through `bg-`, `text-`, and `border-` classes for all components.


**Updating Global Styles**

I'm now focusing on the global style adjustments in `index.html`. Initially, I considered relying on `app-root` for screen styles, but opted to update the `style` tag in `index.html`. This will involve changing the body styles to utilize Tailwind classes directly, specifically for background and text colors. I'll utilize Tailwind CDN's processing of style tags to achieve this, removing hardcoded hex codes for a cleaner approach. My goal is to use `bg-slate-50 dark:bg-slate-950` and related classes.


**Refining Index.html Changes**

I'm now revising `index.html`. My approach involves setting base styles to prevent FOUC. I'll configure the `dark` class on the `html` element. I'm updating `index.html`'s `style` tag, changing body styles to use variables or Tailwind classes. I'll utilize Tailwind's processing of style tags, setting `bg-slate-50 dark:bg-slate-950` and corresponding text colors. Finally, I'll incorporate a dark/light mode toggle via the theme signal.


**Updating Component Styles**

I'm now methodically updating the components mentioned. In `app.component.html`, I'm refining the settings modal and adjusting layout classes from `bg-slate-950` to `bg-slate-50 dark:bg-slate-950`, including borders and text colors. I'm modifying `intel-panel.component.ts` by updating `bg-slate-900/50` to `bg-white/50 dark:bg-slate-900/50` and refining text colors. Table styles are updated in `market-grid.component.ts`, `bg-slate-900` to `bg-slate-100 dark:bg-slate-900`. I'm also addressing row hover colors. In `signal-feed.component.ts`, item backgrounds will be updated.  Finally, `token-detail.component.ts` requires panel backgrounds, metric card styles `bg-slate-900/80` to `bg-white/80 dark:bg-slate-900/80`. Stroke colors are being reviewed in `sync-indicator.component.ts` as well.


**Finalizing Style Updates**

I'm now completing the style adjustments across remaining components, adhering to the established light/dark mode mappings for consistency. I am ensuring class transformations align with the predetermined light/dark color mappings to achieve the desired visual experience. The primary focus is maintaining a clean, predictable implementation and I am reviewing the color scheme in all updated components. I will now integrate the new style changes to complete the code changes required.

To add a robust Dark/Light mode, I will update the Tailwind configuration, the user preferences service, and the styles across all components.

Here are the changes:

1.  **UserPrefsService**: Added `theme` state.
2.  **Index.html**: Configured Tailwind for `darkMode: 'class'` and added CSS variables/styles for both modes.
3.  **App Component**: Added logic to apply the theme class to the `<html>` tag and updated the main layout and Settings modal.
4.  **Components**: Updated all UI components (Grid, Intel, Feed, Detail, Sync, Logo) to support both `dark:` and light mode styles.

entries.description: Add theme signal to UserPrefsService.
Enable Tailwind dark mode (class strategy) and update body styles for dual themes.

diffs.replacement:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en" class="dark">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Via AXIOM</title>
  <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
  <script>
    tailwind.config = {
      darkMode: 'class',
      theme: {
        extend: {
          colors: {
            slate: {
              925: '#0b1120', // Custom deep slate between 900 and 950
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  </script>
  <style>
    /* Custom Scrollbar for Terminal Feel */
    ::-webkit-scrollbar {
      width: 8px;
      height: 8px;
    }
    
    /* Dark Mode Scrollbar */
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-track {
      background: #0f172a; 
    }
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
      background: #1e293b; 
      border: 1px solid #334155;
    }
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb:hover {
      background: #334155; 
    }

    /* Light Mode Scrollbar */
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-track {
      background: #f1f5f9; 
    }
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
      background: #cbd5e1; 
      border: 1px solid #94a3b8;
    }
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb:hover {
      background: #94a3b8; 
    }

    body {
      background-color: #f8fafc; /* Slate 50 */
      color: #0f172a; /* Slate 900 */
      font-family: 'Courier New', Courier, monospace; /* Monospace for terminal vibe */
      transition: background-color 0.5s, color 0.5s;
    }
    
    .dark body {
      background-color: #020617; /* Slate 950 */
      color: #e2e8f0; /* Slate 200 */
    }
    
    /* Branding Classes */
    .axiom-border {
      border: 1px solid #e2e8f0; /* Light mode border */
      box-shadow: 0 4px 6px -1px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
    }
    .dark .axiom-border {
      border: 1px solid #1e293b;
      box-shadow: 0 0 15px rgba(16, 185, 129, 0.05); /* Faint green glow */
    }

    .text-glow {
      text-shadow: none;
    }
    .dark .text-glow {
      text-shadow: 0 0 5px rgba(74, 222, 128, 0.5);
    }

    .axiom-text {
      text-shadow: none;
    }
    .dark .axiom-text {
      text-shadow: 0 0 12px rgba(74, 222, 128, 0.25);
    }

    /* Heartbeat Animation */
    @keyframes heartbeat {
      0% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      5% { transform: scale(1.4); opacity: 0.9; }
      10% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      15% { transform: scale(1.4); opacity: 0.9; }
      50% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      100% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
    }
    .animate-heartbeat {
      animation: heartbeat 2s infinite cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.6, 1);
    }
    
    /* Reduced Motion Overrides */
    .motion-safe .axiom-border {
      box-shadow: none !important;
    }
    .motion-safe .text-glow,
    .motion-safe .axiom-text {
      text-shadow: none !important;
    }
    .motion-safe .animate-heartbeat {
      animation: none !important;
    }
    .motion-safe * {
      animation: none !important;
      transition: none !important;
    }
  </style>
<script type="importmap">
{
  "imports": {
    "rxjs": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/operators": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/operators?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/ajax": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/ajax?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/webSocket": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/webSocket?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/testing": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/testing?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/fetch": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/fetch?conditions=es2015",
    "@angular/common": "https://esm.sh/@angular/common@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/platform-browser": "https://esm.sh/@angular/platform-browser@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/compiler": "https://esm.sh/@angular/compiler@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "@google/genai": "https://esm.sh/@google/genai@^1.38.0?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/core": "https://esm.sh/@angular/core@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "d3": "https://esm.sh/d3@7.9.0?conditions=es2015"
  }
}
</script>
</head>
<body>
  <app-root></app-root>
</body>
</html>

diagnostics.message: Property 'documentElement' does not exist on type 'unknown'.

payload.text:
**Addressing the TypeScript Error**

I'm currently focused on the TypeScript error in `src/app.component.ts`, specifically line 69. The issue, as I understand it, stems from `this.document` being injected using `inject(DOCUMENT)`. The type of `document` is `unknown` by default, causing the "documentElement" property not to be recognized. I'm exploring solutions to properly type the `document` object to fix this.


**Typing the `document` object**

I've realized that the `DOCUMENT` injection token, as used in `src/app.component.ts`, might be the source of the typing ambiguity. The `inject(DOCUMENT)` call may not be inferring the `Document` type automatically in this environment. Therefore, I need to look into explicitly casting `this.document` or explicitly typing the property to resolve the `documentElement` access error. This will allow the type-checker to recognize the property.


**Verifying the Injection**

I've confirmed `inject(DOCUMENT)` usually returns `Document`, but I need to account for possible configuration issues. Strict templates or the `DOCUMENT` type definition might be problematic. I checked the imports and it seems I am importing the `DOCUMENT` object correctly from `@angular/common`. Therefore, I'm confident the correct approach is the type cast, such as `private document = inject(DOCUMENT) as Document;`.


**Reviewing Module Imports**

I'm now reviewing the imports in `app.component.ts`, making sure that all the necessary components and services are correctly declared within the `imports` array. This includes the `CommonModule`, `IntelPanelComponent`, and other relevant UI elements. Ensuring proper import statements is crucial for component integration.


**Applying the Type Assertion**

I'm confident that asserting the type of `this.document` as `Document` will fix the error. The `DOCUMENT` token itself should be typed as `Document`, but the type inference isn't working as expected. Therefore, I will change the injection to `private document = inject(DOCUMENT) as Document;` in `src/app.component.ts`. The referenced `app.component.html` file is not given. I will assume it's correctly linked by the user.


**Refining the Type Assertion**

I'm now more focused on explicitly defining `document`'s type to resolve the TypeScript error. The `inject(DOCUMENT)` call in `src/app.component.ts` isn't inferring the `Document` type, so I need to specify it. payload.text: In dark mode, I don't really care for the green and white contrast.

**Analyzing Dark Mode Contrast**

I'm currently focused on the dark mode color scheme. The primary issue I'm addressing is the user's dislike of the green and white contrast, which seems to stem from `slate-950` backgrounds and light text, often `slate-100` or white. I'm exploring potential adjustments for improved visual comfort.


**Refining Green and White**

I'm now zeroing in on the "green and white contrast" complaint. It appears the issue isn't just a general aversion, but perhaps a specific combination: the bright `green-500` or `green-400` accents against `fill-white` in the logo and white text alongside the dark background. I'm considering softening the "white" to a light slate, inspired by common "hacker" themes, and reducing the neon-like quality of the green. Let me check the impact.


**Adjusting White and Green**

I'm now zeroing in on the specific "green and white contrast" issue, now that I have reviewed the earlier notes. I'm thinking the "White" text should shift to `slate-300` or `slate-200` to mitigate harshness. I will keep the current green accent for now, as it fits the brand, and tweak the white. However, I'm now considering the user might prefer a different accent color entirely, though that wasn't requested. Let me test the current green/white harmony first.


**Modifying Text and Logo**

I'm now implementing the plan. I'm modifying the dark mode text color; specifically, body text is shifting to `slate-400` or `slate-300` and the titles are also slightly muted. Then I'm working on the `green` to try out a darker tone, such as `emerald-500` or `teal-400`. Crucially, I'm addressing the logo, as I am changing the logo's white circle fill to a darker slate or matching the accent.


**Implementing Color Updates**

I've decided to proceed with implementing these color adjustments. I'm swapping the dark mode accent to `emerald-400`, as I feel it is more comfortable on dark backgrounds compared to `green-500`. I'm also softening the text to `slate-200`. For the logo, I'll be changing the white circle fill to `slate-200` in dark mode. Next, I am updating the glow effects to match the new emerald color, and updating components.


**Exploring Alternative Dark Modes**

I'm now thinking the user's "green and white" dislike might stem from a desire for a different color family entirely in dark mode. Considering cyberpunk aesthetics and a data-dense design, I'm now exploring cyan, magenta, or amber as alternative accent colors. I'm currently focusing on cyan (`cyan-400`) and emerald, which are more common in this context, rather than green, which the user dislikes. Let me see what I can make work.


**Refining Accent Choices**

I'm now weighing alternative accents. While teal (`teal-400`) has promise, I think I'll lean towards emerald (`emerald-400`) for the dark mode accent. It's a slightly bluer green, avoiding the harshness of the original. I'm also ensuring the text uses `slate-300`, which should be a softer white. I'm also thinking of how the user could *prefer* a classic terminal scheme, with green text on black.


**Implementing Teal Changes**

I'm now implementing the dark mode color updates across components. I'm focusing on the component HTML, header titles, regime labels, and settings buttons, specifically. I'm also addressing the market grid hover text and signal feed success colors; I'll keep semantic green/red for signal meaning, while shifting UI branding to teal. I'm also updating logos, borders and title text for a unified look.


**Updating Teal Implementations**

I'm now making final updates to integrate Teal-400 into the dark mode. I'm focusing on key components: the app header, logo, and sync indicator. I will ensure that the ring and text are updated, and apply this color to settings buttons and any UI interaction highlights. Notably, I'll switch market grid selection highlights and hover text to teal, to replace the green. Data indicators will remain green to maintain financial meaning.


**Finalizing Teal Implementation**

I'm now making the final changes. I'm updating the `index.html` file to use the teal color for text-glow and border shadows. I'm updating the app and the logo and sync indicators with the `teal-400` color. Also, the market grid, the hover and selection highlights are now using teal instead of the original green. Finally, I updated the settings modal active buttons and input focus to teal-based colors.

To address the contrast issue in dark mode, I will shift the **UI branding and interactive elements** from Green to **Teal** (`teal-400`) in the dark theme. This creates a more sophisticated "cyberpunk/financial" aesthetic that contrasts beautifully with the slate background, while preserving standard **Green/Red** semantics for market data (price changes, buy signals) to maintain clarity.

Here are the updated files.

entries.description: Update dark mode glow effects to Teal to reduce harsh green/white contrast.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en" class="dark">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Via AXIOM</title>
  <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
  <script>
    tailwind.config = {
      darkMode: 'class',
      theme: {
        extend: {
          colors: {
            slate: {
              925: '#0b1120', // Custom deep slate between 900 and 950
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  </script>
  <style>
    /* Custom Scrollbar for Terminal Feel */
    ::-webkit-scrollbar {
      width: 8px;
      height: 8px;
    }
    
    /* Dark Mode Scrollbar */
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-track {
      background: #0f172a; 
    }
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
      background: #1e293b; 
      border: 1px solid #334155;
    }
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb:hover {
      background: #334155; 
    }

    /* Light Mode Scrollbar */
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-track {
      background: #f1f5f9; 
    }
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
      background: #cbd5e1; 
      border: 1px solid #94a3b8;
    }
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb:hover {
      background: #94a3b8; 
    }

    body {
      background-color: #f8fafc; /* Slate 50 */
      color: #0f172a; /* Slate 900 */
      font-family: 'Courier New', Courier, monospace; /* Monospace for terminal vibe */
      transition: background-color 0.5s, color 0.5s;
    }
    
    .dark body {
      background-color: #020617; /* Slate 950 */
      color: #e2e8f0; /* Slate 200 */
    }
    
    /* Branding Classes */
    .axiom-border {
      border: 1px solid #e2e8f0; /* Light mode border */
      box-shadow: 0 4px 6px -1px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
    }
    .dark .axiom-border {
      border: 1px solid #1e293b;
      /* Updated to Teal glow for better contrast */
      box-shadow: 0 0 15px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.05); 
    }

    .text-glow {
      text-shadow: none;
    }
    .dark .text-glow {
      /* Updated to Teal glow */
      text-shadow: 0 0 5px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.5);
    }

    .axiom-text {
      text-shadow: none;
    }
    .dark .axiom-text {
      /* Updated to Teal glow */
      text-shadow: 0 0 12px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.25);
    }

    /* Heartbeat Animation */
    @keyframes heartbeat {
      0% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      5% { transform: scale(1.4); opacity: 0.9; }
      10% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      15% { transform: scale(1.4); opacity: 0.9; }
      50% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      100% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
    }
    .animate-heartbeat {
      animation: heartbeat 2s infinite cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.6, 1);
    }
    
    /* Reduced Motion Overrides */
    .motion-safe .axiom-border {
      box-shadow: none !important;
    }
    .motion-safe .text-glow,
    .motion-safe .axiom-text {
      text-shadow: none !important;
    }
    .motion-safe .animate-heartbeat {
      animation: none !important;
    }
    .motion-safe * {
      animation: none !important;
      transition: none !important;
    }
  </style>
<script type="importmap">
{
  "imports": {
    "rxjs": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/operators": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/operators?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/ajax": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/ajax?conditions=es2015",

    "rxjs/fetch": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/fetch?conditions=es2015",
    "@angular/common": "https://esm.sh/@angular/common@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/platform-browser": "https://esm.sh/@angular/platform-browser@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/compiler": "https://esm.sh/@angular/compiler@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "@google/genai": "https://esm.sh/@google/genai@^1.38.0?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/core": "https://esm.sh/@angular/core@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "d3": "https://esm.sh/d3@7.9.0?conditions=es2015"
  }
}
</script>
</head>
<body>
  <app-root></app-root>
</body>
</html>

payload.text: for "next pulse" can we change "IN" to a "... .. ." animations?

**Revising the UI** I'm considering how to replace the specific second count in the `SyncIndicatorComponent` with an animated ellipsis. My focus is ensuring the "Next Pulse" label remains clear and conveys waiting without the explicit numerical countdown. **Analyzing the Countdown** I've located the `sync-indicator.component.ts` file and reviewed its current HTML implementation, particularly the countdown section. My analysis shows the existing logic uses a `number` pipe for countdown formatting. Replacing this with a dot animation requires a shift away from numerical output. I'm prioritizing keeping the "Next Pulse" label's meaning clear. I am currently trying out various Tailwind animations. **Refining the Animation** I'm now prioritizing simplicity. I've considered several animation ideas for the ellipsis, including a cycling text signal, but I feel it's overkill. I think the user's "... .. ." pattern or something similar is best, but pure CSS text animation is proving tricky. I am currently considering a `[ ... ]` with `animate-pulse`, or just a simple `...` with the same effect, to focus on the waiting context conveyed by the "Next Pulse" label. **Prioritizing Simplicity** I'm now implementing the blinking dot sequence. I'm leaning toward `...` or potentially `WAITING ...`. I'm focusing on the user's "waiting" indication request. I'm also considering a more cyberpunk-inspired approach, replacing the numerical countdown with "SYNCING" and a progress indicator, but the blinking dots seem more straightforward. **Interpreting the Pattern** I'm now focusing on replicating the `... .. .` sequence and interpreting "animations" as "make it look active." I'm prioritizing implementing a "scanning" feel and have replaced the numeric countdown with a new approach. I will try `AWAITING` followed by a blinking cursor block for a terminal-like feel. **Implementing "Standby"** I'm now integrating the Tailwind animation for a "Standby" indication as a direct replacement for the numerical countdown. I'm focusing on the user's need for a less stressful, cleaner visual. I'm verifying the code's location in `src/components/sync-indicator.component.ts` and refining the HTML. I'm also ensuring the animation provides a clear indication of a waiting state. **Implementing the Wave** I've crafted a blinking dot sequence using Tailwind's `animate-pulse` and `delay` utilities to create a wave effect, directly addressing the literal interpretation of "... .. .". The code is now: `...`. I'll also modify the "Next Pulse" label for better context. I'm verifying that the CDN-based Tailwind config supports the specified `delay` values. **Revising "Acquiring..."** I've decided on "ACQUIRING..." with an ellipsis, directly replacing the numerical countdown when updating, and keeping the "... .. ." animation for the waiting state, for simplicity. I'm focusing on the clarity for when the process starts. The label "Next Pulse" stays. The code is implemented and looks good. **Confirming Integration** I've replaced the numeric countdown with animated dots to give a cleaner feel. I'm confirming that the `delay-150` and `delay-300` classes function correctly in standard Tailwind. I'm also addressing animation delays, which require a bit more attention in standard Tailwind, needing either plugins or arbitrary values since `animate-pulse` sets the `animation` property. I'm currently working to confirm that the changes integrate smoothly. **Verifying Delay Implementation** I'm now confident that I can use inline styles with `animation-delay` for consistent results. I'm focusing on ensuring proper animation timing across all environments, and the inline style provides the most robust solution. I need to verify that the pulse animation works correctly using this method, making sure that it runs indefinitely without interruption.

To implement this change, I will update the `SyncIndicatorComponent` to replace the explicit seconds countdown with a rhythmic, pulsing dot animation (`. . .`) that uses staggered animation delays to create a "wave" or "waiting" effect. This aligns with the terminal aesthetic by reducing numerical noise while maintaining the concept of a pending process.

payload.text: ALGORITHM SIGNALS is the main function of this app. Let's expand it to be more of a tool vs just some data. Let's also start to expand the prediction Engine.

**Defining the "Tool"**

I'm currently focused on defining "ALGORITHM SIGNALS" as a genuine "tool" rather than a mere data list, exploring its functionality. Furthermore, I'm working to understand how the "Prediction Engine" can be expanded as well, in order to make it far more capable.


**Expanding the Engine**

I'm now actively deconstructing the "Signals as a Tool" concept. It's moving from a basic list to an interactive system. I'm focusing on features such as filtering, a confidence score, and actionability, which would link signals to deeper token insights. Plus, I want to add signal history visualization for context. Next, I'll focus on the "Prediction Engine" expansion.


**Integrating AI Analysis**

I'm now integrating AI to provide signal-specific assessments. I'm working on enabling Gemini to analyze the context of each signal. Furthermore, I've designed the "Predictor" algorithm to estimate a price target. I am also updating the design to add confidence scoring and a price projection feature, which would be calculated using recent slopes.


**Refining the Interface**

I'm now overhauling the user interface into a full "Signal Terminal." I'm implementing tabs and filters ("ALL," "HIGH CONFIDENCE," "RISK ALERT," "OPPORTUNITY") for improved signal navigation. The list items will feature a confidence bar and an "ANALYZE" button to trigger specific AI analysis from Gemini. Clicking "ANALYZE" will trigger an overlay for a detailed signal assessment. The underlying `GeminiService` is getting a new `analyzeSignalContext` method.


**Developing New Predictions**

I'm now focused on generating a new trend-based signal, the "TREND_PREDICTION." I'll be implementing linear regression on recent data, with a slope threshold. Positive or negative slopes trigger price projection signals for the next timeframe. I'm also updating types.ts to include `confidence` and `aiAnalysis`, and modifying `token-state.service.ts` for improved Burst Hunter confidence.


**Implementing New Analysis**

I'm now integrating the Hype Pulse, boosting confidence based on RSI distance from 70. I'm also adding a `TrendVector` algorithm via linear regression. My next step involves modifying `gemini.service.ts` to include an `analyzeSignal` function. I am working on the heavy-lifting `signal-feed.component.ts` adjustments with filters and expand/analyze states. I'm building out the template with a toolbar, confidence bars, and expand buttons, as well.

This is a significant evolution of the application. We are transforming the **Signals** from a passive feed into an **Interactive Tactical Terminal** and upgrading the **Prediction Engine** with a new algorithm (Linear Regression Trend Projection) and on-demand AI Analysis for specific signals.

Here is the specification for the upgrade:

1.  **Prediction Engine Upgrade (Math & Logic)**:
    *   **New Algorithm: `TREND_VECTOR`**: Uses **Linear Regression** on the last 15 price points to calculate the slope. It projects a "Target Price" for the immediate future.
    *   **Confidence Scoring**: All algorithms now calculate a `confidence` score (0-100%) based on the intensity of the metrics (e.g., an RSI of 85 has higher confidence than 71).

2.  **Signal Terminal (UI/UX)**:
    *   **Filtering & Triage**: Users can now filter signals by category: `HIGH_CONFIDENCE`, `OPPORTUNITY` (Buy/Burst), and `RISK` (Sell/Warning).
    *   **AI Analysis**: Added an **[ANALYZE]** button to each signal. This triggers a specific Gemini request to analyze *that specific signal context* (Price + Volume + Signal Type) and return a tactical prediction.
    *   **Visuals**: Added confidence bars and a cleaner, more dense "data-grid" layout for signals.

Here are the code changes.

To address your request, I have expanded the **Signals Engine** to be a true tactical tool rather than just a data feed, while implementing "logical" interaction rules to prevent accidental UI disruptions.

### 1. New "Logical" Signal Interaction
The user interface for the Signal Feed has been upgraded to prevent accidental clicks.
*   **Problem Solved:** Previously, clicking anywhere on a signal (including the name) might have felt ambiguous or risked triggering unwanted actions.
*   **Solution:** I have decoupled the "Selection" from the "View".
    *   The Token Name is no longer a blind link.
    *   Added a distinct **[OPEN]** button that appears on hover next to the token name. This explicitly opens the Token Detail modal.
    *   Added a **Dismiss (X)** button to each signal, allowing you to clear "noise" from the feed locally.

### 2. Prediction Engine Expansion (Pattern Scanner V2)
I have added a new algorithmic layer to the Prediction Engine in `TokenStateService`.
*   **New Signal Type:** `PATTERN_DETECTED`
*   **Algorithm:** **Support Bounce Detection**. The engine now mathematically analyzes the last 20 periods of price history. It triggers a signal if a token has touched its 20-period low and is currently rebounding with momentum (a classic "Buy the Dip" or "V-Shape" indicator).
*   **Confidence Scoring:** This pattern is weighted based on the sharpness of the rebound.

### 3. Toolset Expansion
*   **New Filters:** Added a `PREDICTION` filter to the signal feed to isolate `TREND_VECTOR` (Linear Regression) and `PATTERN_DETECTED` signals, separating them from standard momentum or volume alerts.

## Code Changes

I'm now integrating a "Batch Analyze" or "Clear All" feature into the action toolbar for better tool-like functionality, and also implementing `VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE` logic in the prediction engine. I will ensure signal selection is separated from viewing in `SignalFeedComponent`. Furthermore, I'll enhance the visibility of the "[OPEN]" button. I'm also ensuring the placeholder status is clear, with "ACQUIRING..." or "MONITORING" shown at appropriate times.


**Refining Status Indicators**

I'm now adding a countdown or progress bar to the "MONITORING" status to make it more informative. I'm also ensuring the "[OPEN]" button is always visible, with a visual clue on hover. I'm adding `VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE` to `SignalType` in `src/services/types.ts` and implementing its logic in `token-state.service.ts`. Finally, I'll add a visual indicator for "Prediction" type signals in `signal-feed.component.ts`.


**Implementing Squeeze Logic**

I'm now incorporating Bollinger Bands and a BandWidth calculation to detect potential "VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE" events, checking if the BandWidth is historically low for a signal. I'm updating `src/components/sync-indicator.component.ts` to improve the "MONITORING" status, potentially with a countdown timer. Furthermore, I'll enhance the "Logical" interactions, updating `signal-feed.component.ts` to implement a distinct "[INSPECT]" button and ensure row clicks don't trigger actions. Finally, I will add an icon for "Squeeze".


**Improving Sync Indicator**

I'm now focusing on a more explicit and persistent "MONITORING" status, especially while the system is waiting. I will enhance the visual feedback to emphasize that the system is active. I will also incorporate VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE logic for the Bollinger Bands and BandWidth. This will involve adding VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE to `src/services/types.ts` and implementing it in `token-state.service.ts`.


**Finalizing Prediction Logic**

I'm now implementing the `VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE` logic using Bollinger Bands and BandWidth. I will calculate `bandwidth` as the difference between the upper and lower bands, divided by the 20-period SMA, and I'm targeting logic for volatility breakout predictions. The plan involves adding a filter in `signal-feed.component.ts` and revising the sync indicator in `sync-indicator.component.ts` for a better user experience. I will then ensure XML validity and correct any detected anti-patterns, including the elimination of regex from templates and the use of the `new Date()` constructor.

This update focuses on three key areas:
1.  **Prediction Engine V2**: Added the **Volatility Squeeze** algorithm. This detects when price action compresses (Bollinger Band Squeeze), which historically precedes an explosive breakout (Prediction).
2.  **Logical Tooling**: The Signal Feed is now strictly "Logical". Clicking a signal card does *nothing* by default to prevent accidents. You must explicitly click the **[OPEN TERMINAL]** button to select the token.
3.  **Enhanced Status**: The Sync Indicator now explicitly communicates its idle state as `AWAITING PULSE` with a visual countdown, satisfying the request for a better placeholder status.

payload.text: Let's do some housekeeping

**Considering Recent Implementations**

I'm currently focused on the broader implications of "housekeeping." I've recently implemented the `VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE` algorithm and updated the `SyncIndicatorComponent`. My attention is now on how these changes relate to ongoing minor enhancements and any potential bug fixes required, considering their effect on the existing codebase.


**Analyzing Code for Consistency**

I'm now diving into the code, starting with `SignalFeedComponent`. I observed a potential inconsistency in how the `inspect` output is handled and will confirm the proper template binding. I verified the `TokenStateService` and ensured the correct imports are included, especially after implementing `VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE`. Finally, I reviewed `gemini.service.ts` and noted the stub `getRSI` method.


**Refining Implementation Accuracy**

I'm now integrating the `MathUtils`'s RSI calculation into `GeminiService.getRSI` to feed more precise data to the AI analysis. The `TokenStateService` now looks correct after the `VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE` implementation. I'm also ensuring overall code cleanliness and addressing any formatting inconsistencies. The `SignalFeedComponent` has a local signal `dismiss` which warrants further inspection.


**Addressing Signal ID Stability**

I'm now focused on signal ID generation within `TokenStateService`. I realized the current use of `Date.now()` leads to unstable IDs, causing dismissed signals to reappear and UI flickering. The solution involves creating stable IDs for the signal's duration, potentially using `timeBucket` or simpler identifiers like `burst-${token.symbol}`. This fix ensures that signals, once dismissed, remain dismissed until the signal condition changes.


**Revising Signal Generation Logic**

I'm now revisiting the signal generation methodology, specifically the ID creation within `TokenStateService`. I realized that, since the condition is re-evaluated every tick, using the event time or a window might be more effective. I considered if the 'burst' approach would work, such as `burst-${token.symbol}`, and realized this would need to reflect signals as transient events. `activeSignals` re-runs every token update, and `Date.now()` leads to unstable IDs and dismissed signal reappearance. I'm focusing on making IDs deterministic and tied to the signal's start.


**Refining Signal Persistence**

I'm now implementing a new signal ID format: `CONDITION_TYPE:SYMBOL`, designed to ensure signal stability and correct dismissal behavior. This approach treats signals as persistent "active conditions," directly addressing the issue of dismissed signals reappearing. This will be the primary focus of my work, and it's essential for preventing UI flickering. I'm focusing on ensuring `TokenStateService` properly reflects this new ID methodology.


**Executing Housekeeping Tasks**

I've decided to refine the signal ID strategy within `TokenStateService`. The initial approach of using `Date.now()` created instability. The revised ID format is now `BURST_HUNTER:${token.symbol}`, ensuring consistent dismissal behavior. This resolves dismissed signals reappearing, regardless of the timestamp update. I am also implementing `getRSI` properly in `GeminiService` and cleaning up `src/app.component.html` and `src/components/market-grid.component.ts`. The focus is now on integrating this new signal ID format and applying it across the codebase.


**Verifying Logic Consistency**

I'm now confirming the new signal ID format implementation in `TokenStateService`, verifying stability. The format will be `TYPE:${token.symbol}`, and it's essential for preventing DOM re-creation and improving performance. I have checked how Angular's `@for` loop and track function are affected by these stable IDs. These improvements are crucial for optimizing UI performance and guaranteeing proper signal dismissal.


**Implementing Stable Signal IDs**

I'm now implementing the stable signal IDs. I'll use the format `ANCHOR:${token.symbol}`, `VENUE:${token.symbol}`, `PATTERN:${token.symbol}`, `SQUEEZE:${token.symbol}` and, lastly, `TREND_VECTOR:${token.symbol}`. The plan is to create stable IDs that will allow Angular to update the message binding inside the existing component instead of re-creating the DOM element every time. I also double-checked and added `MathUtils` to `GeminiService` for proper RSI calculations, and checked `market-simulator.service.ts` for errors. The `VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE` implementation is solid.

This housekeeping update focuses on stability, performance, and data accuracy.

**Key Improvements:**
1.  **Stable Signal IDs (Critical Fix):** Previously, signal IDs included `Date.now()`, causing them to change every millisecond. This meant:
    *   The "Dismiss" button didn't work (dismissing ID `A` didn't stop ID `B` from appearing the next tick).
    *   The UI was re-rendering DOM elements unnecessarily (flashing).
    *   **Fix:** Signal IDs are now deterministic (e.g., `BURST:SOL`). This stabilizes the DOM, improves performance, and ensures "Dismiss" works permanently for an active condition.
2.  **AI Context Accuracy:** The Gemini Service now correctly calculates RSI using `MathUtils` before sending it to the AI, ensuring tactical analysis is based on real math, not "N/A".
3.  **Code Cleanup:** Removed transient timestamp logic that caused jitter.

diffs.replacement:
import { Component, output } from '@angular/core'; import { CommonModule } from '@angular/common'; interface AlgoDoc { id: string; name: string; sub: string; logic: string; math: string; color: string; } @Component({ selector: 'app-algorithm-docs', standalone: true, imports: [CommonModule], template: `

// ALGORITHMIC BLUEPRINT

LOGIC GATES & SIGNAL DEFINITIONS

@for (algo of algorithms; track algo.id) {

{{ algo.name }}

{{ algo.sub }}
ID: {{ algo.id }}
Logic Gate:

{{ algo.logic }}

Arithmetic: {{ algo.math }}
}

SYSTEM NOTE: All signals are probabilistic. No algorithm guarantees future performance. Execution is local.

` }) export class AlgorithmDocsComponent { close = output(); algorithms: AlgoDoc[] = [ { id: '01', name: 'BURST HUNTER', sub: 'CONVICTION DETECTOR', logic: 'Triggers when volume spikes significantly above the moving average AND price creates a strong directional move.', math: 'Vol > SMA_20(Vol) * 3.0 AND Price_Change > 2%', color: 'text-green-600 dark:text-teal-400' }, { id: '02', name: 'VOLATILITY SQUEEZE', sub: 'PREDICTION ENGINE V3', logic: 'Identifies periods of extremely low volatility (Bollinger Band Squeeze), which mathematically precede explosive breakouts.', math: 'Bandwidth = (UpperBB - LowerBB) / SMA_20 < 0.01', color: 'text-purple-600 dark:text-purple-400' }, { id: '03', name: 'GAIN LOCK-100', sub: 'RISK MANAGEMENT', logic: 'Advises exit when a position is up >100% and short-term momentum crosses below long-term trend (Death Cross).', math: 'Profit > 100% AND SMA_10 < SMA_50', color: 'text-yellow-600 dark:text-yellow-400' }, { id: '04', name: 'TREND VECTOR', sub: 'LINEAR REGRESSION', logic: 'Calculates the slope of the last 15 price points to project the likely price 5 periods into the future.', math: 'y = mx + b on Price[-15...0]; Project(t+5)', color: 'text-blue-600 dark:text-blue-400' }, { id: '05', name: 'HYPE PULSE', sub: 'MOMENTUM GAUGE', logic: 'Uses RSI to detect unsustainable market exuberance (Overbought) or panic (Oversold).', math: 'RSI(14) > 70 (Overbought) OR RSI(14) < 30 (Oversold)', color: 'text-pink-600 dark:text-pink-400' }, { id: '06', name: 'PATTERN SCANNER', sub: 'GEOMETRIC RECOGNITION', logic: 'Detects V-Shape recoveries by identifying price rebounds from a local 20-period low.', math: '(Price - Min_20) / Min_20 < 0.05 AND Price > Prev_Price', color: 'text-indigo-600 dark:text-indigo-400' }, { id: '07', name: 'STABLE ANCHOR', sub: 'CAPITAL PRESERVATION', logic: 'Identifies assets with low volatility and acceptable yield for safe capital parking.', math: 'StdDev(Price, 20) / Price < 0.05 AND APY > 5%', color: 'text-cyan-600 dark:text-cyan-400' }, { id: '08', name: 'VENUE SHIELD', sub: 'EXECUTION OPTIMIZER', logic: 'Warns against trading assets with insufficient liquidity depth to prevent slippage.', math: 'Liquidity_Depth < $1,000,000 USD', color: 'text-red-600 dark:text-red-400' } ]; }

diffs.replacement:
import { Component, output } from '@angular/core'; import { CommonModule } from '@angular/common'; import { AlgorithmIconComponent, AlgoIconType } from './algorithm-icon.component'; interface AlgoDoc { id: string; name: string; iconName: AlgoIconType; sub: string; logic: string; math: string; color: string; } @Component({ selector: 'app-algorithm-docs', standalone: true, imports: [CommonModule, AlgorithmIconComponent], template: `

// ALGORITHMIC BLUEPRINT

LOGIC GATES & SIGNAL DEFINITIONS

@for (algo of algorithms; track algo.id) {

{{ algo.name }}

{{ algo.sub }}
ID: {{ algo.id }}
Logic Gate:

{{ algo.logic }}

Arithmetic: {{ algo.math }}
}

SYSTEM NOTE: All signals are probabilistic. No algorithm guarantees future performance. Execution is local.

` }) export class AlgorithmDocsComponent { close = output(); algorithms: AlgoDoc[] = [ { id: '01', name: 'BURST HUNTER', iconName: 'BURST_HUNTER', sub: 'CONVICTION DETECTOR', logic: 'Triggers when volume spikes significantly above the moving average AND price creates a strong directional move.', math: 'Vol > SMA_20(Vol) * 3.0 AND Price_Change > 2%', color: 'text-green-600 dark:text-teal-400' }, { id: '02', name: 'VOLATILITY SQUEEZE', iconName: 'VOLATILITY_SQUEEZE', sub: 'PREDICTION ENGINE V3', logic: 'Identifies periods of extremely low volatility (Bollinger Band Squeeze), which mathematically precede explosive breakouts.', math: 'Bandwidth = (UpperBB - LowerBB) / SMA_20 < 0.01', color: 'text-purple-600 dark:text-purple-400' }, { id: '03', name: 'GAIN LOCK-100', iconName: 'GAIN_LOCK', sub: 'RISK MANAGEMENT', logic: 'Advises exit when a position is up >100% and short-term momentum crosses below long-term trend (Death Cross).', math: 'Profit > 100% AND SMA_10 < SMA_50', color: 'text-yellow-600 dark:text-yellow-400' }, { id: '04', name: 'TREND VECTOR', iconName: 'TREND_VECTOR', sub: 'LINEAR REGRESSION', logic: 'Calculates the slope of the last 15 price points to project the likely price 5 periods into the future.', math: 'y = mx + b on Price[-15...0]; Project(t+5)', color: 'text-blue-600 dark:text-blue-400' }, { id: '05', name: 'HYPE PULSE', iconName: 'HYPE_PULSE', sub: 'MOMENTUM GAUGE', logic: 'Uses RSI to detect unsustainable market exuberance (Overbought) or panic (Oversold).', math: 'RSI(14) > 70 (Overbought) OR RSI(14) < 30 (Oversold)', color: 'text-pink-600 dark:text-pink-400' }, { id: '06', name: 'PATTERN SCANNER', iconName: 'PATTERN_SCANNER', sub: 'GEOMETRIC RECOGNITION', logic: 'Detects V-Shape recoveries by identifying price rebounds from a local 20-period low.', math: '(Price - Min_20) / Min_20 < 0.05 AND Price > Prev_Price', color: 'text-indigo-600 dark:text-indigo-400' }, { id: '07', name: 'STABLE ANCHOR', iconName: 'STABLE_ANCHOR', sub: 'CAPITAL PRESERVATION', logic: 'Identifies assets with low volatility and acceptable yield for safe capital parking.', math: 'StdDev(Price, 20) / Price < 0.05 AND APY > 5%', color: 'text-cyan-600 dark:text-cyan-400' }, { id: '08', name: 'VENUE SHIELD', iconName: 'VENUE_SHIELD', sub: 'EXECUTION OPTIMIZER', logic: 'Warns against trading assets with insufficient liquidity depth to prevent slippage.', math: 'Liquidity_Depth < $1,000,000 USD', color: 'text-red-600 dark:text-red-400' } ]; }

payload.text: Implement a feature in the `TokenStateService` that allows users to set price alerts for specific tokens. When a token's price crosses a set threshold, trigger a visual notification or a distinct signal in the feed.

**Defining Alert Structure**

I'm working on the initial data structure. I'm focusing on how to represent price alerts. Each alert will need a symbol, a target price, and a condition (above or below). It's crucial this data is structured efficiently for easy access and manipulation later on. I'm leaning toward a specific format to keep things streamlined.


**Structuring Alert Logic**

I've outlined the core data structure: a `Map` within `TokenStateService` to store alerts, keyed by symbol. Each alert will be an object with symbol, target price, condition, and triggered status. Methods for setting and removing alerts are planned. Setting a zero or null price will remove the alert. The main signal will incorporate checks for active alerts.


**Implementing Alert Logic**

I've got the data structure and state management sorted. Now, I'm integrating the alert logic into the `activeSignals` computation. I'm focusing on ensuring each alert triggers only once, using the `triggered` flag, and resetting that flag when new alerts are set. The `setPriceAlert` method now handles the `triggered` flag reset. The integration with the token price data seems very straightforward and clean.


**Planning Alert Feature**

I'm solidifying the implementation plan. I've sketched the data structures, signal types, and user interface elements. I will create a `TokenStateService` and a `PriceAlert` interface for storing alerts. I have decided to add methods to set and remove alerts within `TokenStateService`. I will introduce a signal for price alerts in `TokenStateService`. I plan to make the triggered property of the price alert false, when setting a new alert for the same symbol. The plan for the new UI is coming together. I will adjust the UI components to support this.



**Defining Alert Structure**

I'm solidifying the data structures for the price alert feature. I plan to use a `Map` within `TokenStateService` for easy alert lookups by symbol. A `PriceAlert` interface will define the alert structure, including target price, condition, and a trigger flag to prevent repeated alerts. I will also incorporate UI components to handle the user interactions for creating and removing alerts.



**Refining Alert Implementation**

I'm now focusing on integrating the alert logic within the existing reactive data flow. I will add a separate computed signal for price alerts and merge it with `activeSignals`. I'll handle the `triggered` state mutation when the alert conditions are evaluated within `TokenStateService`, but NOT inside the computed. I also plan to have the checks happen in the `updateTokens` method. This approach ensures reactivity and addresses side-effect concerns. Finally, I will address the UI changes.


**Refining Alert Implementation**

I've decided to refine the implementation to keep `activeSignals` pure. I will create a separate computed signal for price alerts and merge it with `activeSignals`. The alert condition check will happen in the `updateTokens` method, and a new signal will be created when an alert is triggered. Triggered state will now be handled within the signal itself, and the `SignalFeedComponent` will dismiss the alerts.


**Refining Alert Strategy**

I've decided to proceed with the revised `TokenStateService` plan. No 'triggered' flag will be used. Instead, the `SignalFeedComponent` will manage dismissals. I'm focusing on defining the data structure for price alerts, which now excludes the `triggered` flag, and also implementing `setPriceAlert` and `removePriceAlert` methods within the service. This approach simplifies the reactivity and avoids state mutation issues. I will now adjust UI components to support setting and removing alerts.


**Implementing Price Alert**

I've refactored the approach, removing the `triggered` flag. Instead, the `SignalFeedComponent` will now handle signal dismissal. I've updated the `TokenStateService` to remove the `triggered` state mutation. `TokenStateService` will now just check the conditions of the price alerts, and create the signal. I will now adjust UI components to support setting and removing alerts.


**Refining Alert Strategy**

I've moved forward, and I'm updating my approach to eliminate the 'triggered' flag. The `SignalFeedComponent` will manage signal dismissal. Now I'm focused on the data structure for price alerts. I'm implementing the `setPriceAlert` and `removePriceAlert` methods in the service. The goal is to simplify reactivity, avoiding state issues. I will now adjust UI components to support setting and removing alerts.


**Implementing Price Alert**

I've made significant progress implementing the price alert feature. The core logic now resides within the `TokenStateService`. I'm using a reactive `priceAlerts` signal to manage alert configurations. The `setPriceAlert` and `removePriceAlert` methods are implemented. I've integrated these alerts within the `activeSignals` computation, and the logic is integrated with existing data flows.


**Implementing Price Alert**

I've refactored and finished implementing the core price alert functionality. I've successfully added a `priceAlerts` signal within the `TokenStateService`. The logic for setting, removing, and checking alerts is now integrated. I'm leveraging the `activeSignals` computation and have the UI components coming together nicely. I also added a "warning" severity and signal filtering to `SignalFeedComponent`.

Of course. I will implement a price alert system that allows you to set a price target for any token directly from its detail panel. When the token's price crosses your target, a high-priority signal will be generated in the signal feed.

Here is a summary of the changes:

1.  **New Signal Type**: I've introduced a `PRICE_ALERT` signal type. These signals are classified as 'warning' severity and will appear in the "RISK ALERT" filter category.
2.  **State Management**: The `TokenStateService` now tracks all active price alerts. The core signal generation logic has been updated to continuously check token prices against these user-defined alerts.
3.  **Token Detail UI**: The token detail modal now features a new **Price Alert** section.
    *   If no alert is set, you will see a simple form to input a target price, select whether to be alerted "Above" or "Below" that price, and set the alert.
    *   If an alert is active, the UI will display the current target and provide a button to clear it.

This feature gives you a powerful tool to monitor specific price levels without having to constantly watch the market grid.

entries.description: Add PRICE_ALERT to SignalType enum and define a PriceAlert interface.
Add state and methods for managing price alerts and integrate alert checking into the signal generation logic.
Implement UI and logic for setting and clearing price alerts within the Token Detail modal.

payload.text: For "enter target price", please give a few logically defaults to choose from too. allow "%" along with price.

**Developing Price Alerts**

I'm working on enhancing the "Price Alert" feature in the `TokenDetailComponent`. My focus is currently on providing a better initial experience. I'm considering offering default price targets as quick-selection buttons. I also think allowing percentage-based alerts is a good idea. This allows for a more flexible and user-friendly system, I think.


**Implementing Default Targets**

I've made progress on the price alert feature. I've created the `computed` signal to calculate default price targets, including +/- 5% and +/- 10% from the current price. I'm now working on the component's template, adding buttons for these defaults to populate the price input. The plan is also to handle percentage inputs correctly in the `setAlert` logic.


**Refining UI Interactions**

I'm now integrating those default price target buttons into the component's template. I've focused on visual design, making the buttons look like small, rounded pills. The placeholder text of the input field has been updated to provide hints about the new percentage input feature. I'm making sure these UI changes integrate well with the existing design.


**Implementing Percentage Alerts**

I've completed the core logic for percentage-based alerts within the `setAlert` method. I've successfully implemented parsing percentage inputs (e.g., +5%, -10%), calculating target prices relative to the current token price, and setting the alert condition. The logic now accounts for both positive and negative percentages. Next I will refactor the unit tests, and I'll revisit error handling.


**Refining Alert Condition Handling**

I'm now revising the `setAlert` method. I've realized that percentage inputs require rethinking the 'above'/'below' condition selection. I'm focusing on making the logic intuitive and user-friendly for both percentage and absolute price inputs. My goal is to determine the appropriate condition based on percentage sign, and absolute input. I'm still working out the ideal implementation.


**Refining Percentage Handling**

I'm now implementing the refined logic for the `setAlert` method. I'm focusing on handling percentage inputs correctly, so alerts work as expected. I'm now ensuring the correct `ABOVE` or `BELOW` conditions, based on the input percentage. I'll test the percentage handling, as well as absolute price inputs, after this refinement.


**Finalizing Logic Refinement**

I've completed the revised `setAlert` method. It correctly handles both percentage inputs (like "+5%" and "-10%") and absolute prices, while also respecting the user-selected "ABOVE" or "BELOW" alert condition. I've also integrated the alert suggestion buttons, and updated the placeholder text. Now I need to write unit tests, and I'll revisit error handling.


**Integrating Quick Set Buttons**

I've been working to integrate the suggestion buttons directly into the `TokenDetailComponent`. I've combined the `selectSuggestion` functionality with alert setting. Now, each button click will directly set the corresponding price alert. I've also kept the input for custom price or percentage entries. The UI tweaks are complete, including the placeholder update, and the new quick set buttons.

**Refining Logic Integration**

I've streamlined the interaction of quick-set buttons and the input field. Now, selecting a suggestion button directly sets the price alert, simplifying the user flow. The UI has been tweaked, including the placeholder update, and the quick set buttons are integrated. I'm focusing on writing unit tests now. Error handling is the next focus.


**Finalizing Percentage Input**

I'm now writing unit tests and error handling for the price alert feature. I'm focusing on validating the percentage input and making sure alerts are correctly triggered with both absolute and percentage values, as well as checking the interaction of quick set buttons. I am also working on testing the integration with the `StateService`.


**Refining Component Integration**

I'm now writing comprehensive unit tests for the percentage input, including positive and negative scenarios and error handling. I'm verifying interaction with the quick set buttons. I am also testing integration with the `StateService` to ensure alerts are set and updated correctly.


**Refining Logic Integration**

I've integrated the quick-set suggestion buttons directly into the component and streamlined alert setting. Clicking these buttons immediately sets the alert, improving the user experience. The UI has been updated to include the quick set buttons, and the placeholder has been updated. I'm focusing on unit tests, now, and error handling.


**Refining Implementation Integration**

I'm now writing and testing the unit tests. I'm focusing on validating the interaction of quick set buttons, which directly set alerts. I'm verifying the input parsing for percentage values and absolute prices, ensuring alerts are triggered correctly. I'm testing error handling and edge cases in the component's integration with the `StateService`.

Of course. I've enhanced the price alert system to be more intuitive and powerful, incorporating your suggestions for default targets and percentage-based inputs.

Here are the specific upgrades I've made to the token detail panel:

1.  **Quick-Set Alerts**: Below the manual input, you will now find four "Quick Set" buttons: `+5%`, `-5%`, `+10%`, and `-10%`. Clicking any of these will instantly set a price alert at the corresponding level relative to the current price, streamlining the process for common scenarios.
2.  **Percentage-Based Input**: The manual input field now accepts percentage values. You can type `+2.5%` or `-8%` directly into the box. The system will automatically calculate the target price and determine the correct alert condition (`ABOVE` for positive percentages, `BELOW` for negative).
3.  **Smarter UI**: When you use a percentage-based input, the system automatically infers your intent, so you don't need to manually select the "ABOVE" or "BELOW" condition. For absolute price inputs, the toggle buttons still function as before, giving you full control.

These changes make setting precise, relative price targets much faster and more user-friendly.

Excellent suggestion. A "heartbeat" animation on the chart will visually reinforce the live data connection and enhance the terminal's high-tech aesthetic.

I've implemented this feature by adding a subtle, glowing pulse effect to the line graph in the token detail view. Here’s how it works:

1.  **CSS Animation**: I've added new CSS keyframe animations to `index.html` that create a "heartbeat" effect by gently animating the `stroke-width` and a `drop-shadow` glow of the SVG line. The animation has distinct color schemes for both light and dark themes.
2.  **Conditional Activation**: The animation is only active under specific conditions:
    *   It engages only when the data link is active (`isUpdating()`), providing clear visual feedback that you're seeing the latest data tick.
    *   It respects your accessibility settings and will be automatically disabled if "Reduced Motion" is turned on in the system configuration.
3.  **Component Update**: The `TokenDetailComponent` has been updated to inject the `UserPrefsService` and apply the new animation class conditionally.

The result is a dynamic and responsive chart that feels more alive. You can see the effect by opening any token detail panel; the heartbeat will pulse in sync with the data updates from the main sync indicator.

entries.description: Add CSS keyframes and classes for a new "heartbeat" glow animation for the line chart, with variants for both light and dark themes.

diffs.replacement:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en" class="dark">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Via AXIOM</title>
  <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
  <script>
    tailwind.config = {
      darkMode: 'class',
      theme: {
        extend: {
          colors: {
            slate: {
              925: '#0b1120', // Custom deep slate between 900 and 950
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  </script>
  <style>
    /* Custom Scrollbar for Terminal Feel */
    ::-webkit-scrollbar {
      width: 8px;
      height: 8px;
    }
    
    /* Dark Mode Scrollbar */
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-track {
      background: #0f172a; 
    }
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
      background: #1e293b; 
      border: 1px solid #334155;
    }
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb:hover {
      background: #334155; 
    }

    /* Light Mode Scrollbar */
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-track {
      background: #f1f5f9; 
    }
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
      background: #cbd5e1; 
      border: 1px solid #94a3b8;
    }
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb:hover {
      background: #94a3b8; 
    }

    body {
      background-color: #f8fafc; /* Slate 50 */
      color: #0f172a; /* Slate 900 */
      font-family: 'Courier New', Courier, monospace; /* Monospace for terminal vibe */
      transition: background-color 0.5s, color 0.5s;
    }
    
    .dark body {
      background-color: #020617; /* Slate 950 */
      color: #e2e8f0; /* Slate 200 */
    }
    
    /* Branding Classes */
    .axiom-border {
      border: 1px solid #e2e8f0; /* Light mode border */
      box-shadow: 0 4px 6px -1px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
    }
    .dark .axiom-border {
      border: 1px solid #1e293b;
      /* Updated to Teal glow for better contrast */
      box-shadow: 0 0 15px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.05); 
    }

    .text-glow {
      text-shadow: none;
    }
    .dark .text-glow {
      /* Updated to Teal glow */
      text-shadow: 0 0 5px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.5);
    }

    .axiom-text {
      text-shadow: none;
    }
    .dark .axiom-text {
      /* Updated to Teal glow */
      text-shadow: 0 0 12px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.25);
    }

    /* Heartbeat Animation */
    @keyframes heartbeat {
      0% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      5% { transform: scale(1.4); opacity: 0.9; }
      10% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      15% { transform: scale(1.4); opacity: 0.9; }
      50% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      100% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
    }
    .animate-heartbeat {
      animation: heartbeat 2s infinite cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.6, 1);
    }
    
    /* Reduced Motion Overrides */
    .motion-safe .axiom-border {
      box-shadow: none !important;
    }
    .motion-safe .text-glow,
    .motion-safe .axiom-text {
      text-shadow: none !important;
    }
    .motion-safe .animate-heartbeat {
      animation: none !important;
    }
    .motion-safe * {
      animation: none !important;
      transition: none !important;
    }

    /* Chart Heartbeat Animation */
    @keyframes heartbeat-glow-light {
       0%, 100% {
        filter: drop-shadow(0 0 1px rgba(34, 197, 94, 0.6)); /* Green glow */
        stroke-width: 0.6;
      }
      50% {
        filter: drop-shadow(0 0 5px rgba(34, 197, 94, 1));
        stroke-width: 0.8;
      }
    }
    .animate-chart-heartbeat {
       /* Prevent transition from interfering with animation */
       transition-property: stroke, fill, filter; 
       animation: heartbeat-glow-light 2.5s infinite ease-in-out;
    }

    @keyframes heartbeat-glow-dark {
      0%, 100% {
        filter: drop-shadow(0 0 2px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.7)); /* Teal glow */
        stroke-width: 0.6;
      }
      50% {
        filter: drop-shadow(0 0 6px rgba(45, 212, 191, 1));
        stroke-width: 0.8;
      }
    }
    .dark .animate-chart-heartbeat {
        animation-name: heartbeat-glow-dark;
    }
  </style>
<script type="importmap">
{
  "imports": {
    "rxjs": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/operators": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/operators?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/ajax": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/ajax?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/webSocket": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/webSocket?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/testing": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/testing?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/fetch": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/fetch?conditions=es2015",
    "@angular/common": "https://esm.sh/@angular/common@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/platform-browser": "https://esm.sh/@angular/platform-browser@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/compiler": "https://esm.sh/@angular/compiler@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "@google/genai": "https://esm.sh/@google/genai@^1.38.0?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/core": "https://esm.sh/@angular/core@^21.1.1?external=rxjs",
    "d3": "https://esm.sh/d3@7.9.0?conditions=es2015"
  }
}
</script>
</head>
<body>
  <app-root></app-root>
</body>
</html>

  <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
  <script>
    tailwind.config = {
      darkMode: 'class',
      theme: {
        extend: {
          colors: {
            slate: {
              925: '#0b1120', // Custom deep slate between 900 and 950
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  </script>
  <style>
    /* Custom Scrollbar for Terminal Feel */
    ::-webkit-scrollbar {
      width: 8px;
      height: 8px;
    }
    
    /* Dark Mode Scrollbar */
    .dark ::-webkit-scrollbar-track {

    }

      background: #94a3b8; 
    }

    body {
      background-color: #f8fafc; /* Slate 50 */
      color: #0f172a; /* Slate 900 */
      font-family: 'Courier New', Courier, monospace; /* Monospace for terminal vibe */
      transition: background-color 0.5s, color 0.5s;
    }
    
    .dark body {
      background-color: #020617; /* Slate 950 */
      color: #e2e8f0; /* Slate 200 */
    }
    
    /* Branding Classes */
    .axiom-border {
      border: 1px solid #e2e8f0; /* Light mode border */
      box-shadow: 0 4px 6px -1px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
    }
    .dark .axiom-border {
      border: 1px solid #1e293b;
      /* Updated to Teal glow for better contrast */
      box-shadow: 0 0 15px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.05); 
    }

    .text-glow {
      text-shadow: none;
    }
    .dark .text-glow {
      /* Updated to Teal glow */
      text-shadow: 0 0 5px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.5);
    }

    .axiom-text {
      text-shadow: none;
    }
    .dark .axiom-text {
      /* Updated to Teal glow */
      text-shadow: 0 0 12px rgba(45, 212, 191, 0.25);
    }

    /* Heartbeat Animation */
    @keyframes heartbeat {
      0% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      5% { transform: scale(1.4); opacity: 0.9; }
      10% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      15% { transform: scale(1.4); opacity: 0.9; }
      50% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
      100% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
    }
    .animate-heartbeat {

    .motion-safe * {

      transition: none !important;
    }

    /* Chart Scanline "Heartbeat" Animation */
    @keyframes scanline {
      from { transform: translateX(-100%); }
      to { transform: translateX(100%); }
    }

    .scanline-effect {
      position: relative;
      overflow: hidden;
    }

    .scanline-effect::after {
      content: '';
      position: absolute;
      top: 0;
      left: 0;
      width: 100%;
      height: 100%;
      background: linear-gradient(
        to right,
        transparent 0%,
        transparent 45%,
        rgba(34, 197, 94, 0.05) 48%,
        rgba(34, 197, 94, 0.6) 50%,
        rgba(34, 197, 94, 0.05) 52%,

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en" class="dark">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Via AXIOM</title>
  <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
  <script>
    tailwind.config = {
      darkMode: 'class',
      theme: {
        extend: {
          colors: {
            slate: {
              925: '#0b1120', // Custom deep slate between 900 and 950
            }
          }
        }
      }
